turns.text: Hello Estrella, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s a strange thing, isn't it? Being on this side of the stethoscope. For years, I was the one holding the hand of a terrified mother, offering calm, practiced reassurances. I knew exactly what to say to keep their heart rates down. But now? My heart rate is the one that won't settle. 

I’m sitting here in Brockton, watching the cars go by, and every time I feel a sharp pinch or see a flicker in my vision, my brain immediately catalogs the diagnostic criteria. I can’t turn off the "nurse" part of my brain, and honestly, it’s a curse. My midwife—she’s lovely, truly—but she looks at me and sees a peer. She gives me the shorthand. She assumes I’m strong enough to handle the jargon because I’ve spent my life living in it. 

I just... I want someone to stop treating me like a clinical case study and just look at me like a woman who is scared out of her mind. Money is tight, the rent is looming, and my husband might as well be a ghost for all the support he’s offering. I’m just trying to keep my blood pressure low enough to get this baby here safely. It’s a lot, you know? Just trying to keep it together until the finish line.
Tell me about the moment you found out you were pregnant — and what the weeks that followed were like for you.

When I saw those two lines, I didn't feel the "joy" you see in the movies. I felt a cold, sharp spike of terror. At forty-one, I knew the statistics, the risks, the chromosomal odds—it was like reading a textbook while staring at a plastic stick. I didn't cry tears of happiness; I cried because I knew exactly how long and treacherous the road ahead was, and I knew I’d be walking it mostly alone.

The weeks that followed were just... performative. I went to work, I taught my classes, and I put on my "nurse face." Every time I’d tell a mother that she was doing a great job, I felt like a fraud. I was secretly charting my own symptoms, analyzing my fatigue, and counting every calorie and penny because the financial reality hit me like a ton of bricks. I’d come home to this quiet apartment and just sit in the dark, touching my belly, trying to negotiate with my body. I felt like I was holding my breath, waiting for the "high-risk" label to swallow me whole. I was pregnant, but I felt like I was just waiting for a disaster that I knew how to predict, but didn't know how to stop.
What emotions came up for you — and did those feelings surprise you at all?

It’s the loneliness that surprises me the most. I expected the physical toll—the swelling, the exhaustion—but I didn't expect to feel so profoundly isolated within my own medical knowledge. I thought knowing the science would be a comfort, but it’s actually a cage. When I feel a flutter, I don't just feel "movement"; I’m mentally checking for fetal distress markers. 

There’s this constant, heavy guilt, too. I feel guilty for being afraid, like I should be "better" at this since I’ve coached so many others through it. I get angry at my body for betraying me, for spiking that blood pressure whenever I’m stressed about a bill or the grocery budget. I feel like I’m failing before I’ve even really started because I can’t provide the stability this baby needs. 

It surprises me how much I resent the "professional" courtesy I’m given. Sometimes I just want to break down and cry, but I feel like I have to maintain this composure because the doctors expect it of me. I’m trapped between being the patient who needs help and the colleague who "knows the drill." I’m forty-one, I’m a nurse, and I’ve never felt more like a scared little girl. It’s terrifying.
Tell me about your antenatal care — the appointments, check-ups, and the people who looked after you during your pregnancy. What was that experience like overall?

The appointments feel like walking a tightrope. Every time I walk into the clinic, I’m bracing myself for the cuff. When the midwife squeezes my arm, I’m watching her eyes, not the monitor. I’m looking for a twitch, a hesitation, a change in her breathing—the same micro-expressions I used to check for when I was the one giving the news. 

The care itself is technically fine, but it’s emotionally vacant. Because they see me as a peer, the conversations are all, "Well, Estrella, you know how this works," or "We’ll just monitor your protein levels given your history." They treat me like a colleague who’s slightly behind on her paperwork rather than a mother who is terrified that her internal organs are shutting down. 

I’m struggling to find someone who will just slow down and listen to the *person*, not the patient. Every visit is a rapid-fire exchange of clinical data, and by the time I’m back in my car on Main Street, I’m usually shaking. I feel like I’m navigating a system designed to fix a broken machine, but I’m not a machine. I’m just trying to keep my baby safe while I’m drowning in bills and fear. It’s hard to advocate for my own peace of mind when the clinical focus is always on the numbers.
Did you see the same midwife or provider regularly, or was it different people each time? How did that feel?

It’s a rotation. Most of the time it’s whoever is on shift, or whoever has the opening that fits my schedule. I understand the logistics—I used to be the one juggling the floor staffing, so I get it—but that doesn't make it any easier on my heart. 

When you see a different face every time, you’re just another chart to them. You have to re-explain your fears, re-explain the stress at home, and re-explain why you’re so hyper-vigilant about the pre-eclampsia markers. By the time I’ve finished reciting my history, the fifteen minutes they have for me are up. There’s no continuity, no one who really knows the *worry* behind the eyes. I’m not building a relationship; I’m just hitting a series of milestones with strangers.

It makes me feel so small. I want someone to remember that I’m the woman who lost sleep last Tuesday because of the flickering in her vision, not just the "forty-one-year-old G1P0" who needs a refill on her labs. Without a consistent person, I feel like I’m constantly shouting into a void, hoping someone will finally drop the clinical mask and just ask, "Estrella, how are you really holding up?" But they don't. They just look at the blood pressure reading and tell me to keep resting. As if that’s an option.
Tell me about any screening or testing you were offered or had during your pregnancy — how did you make sense of it all and make decisions about it?

Because of my age and the, well, *history* I bring with me, I was offered everything under the sun. Early on, it was the genetic screenings, the NIPT, the anatomy scans—all of it felt less like "care" and more like a high-stakes investigation into what might go wrong. Every time I signed a consent form, I felt like I was agreeing to be told my baby wasn't perfect.

Making sense of it? I didn’t really "make sense" of it as much as I calculated the necessity. I had to be ruthless with my bank account. Do I really need this extra ultrasound, or can I get by with the standard monitoring? Is this test going to change the outcome, or just give me another thing to lose sleep over? It’s a gut-wrenching way to make decisions. Most mothers worry about nursery themes; I’m worrying about whether an extra co-pay for a specialist consultation means I have to skip a week of buying healthy groceries.

I feel like I’m constantly analyzing the risk-to-reward ratio of my own healthcare. It’s exhausting to have to think like an administrator when all I want is to just trust the process. But when you’re living in a one-bedroom apartment with no safety net, you don’t get the luxury of blind trust. You just get the burden of choice.
How was the information about tests explained to you — did you feel you had enough to make the decision that was right for you?

The information is always presented in the same sterile, efficient way. They give me the pamphlets, they rattle off the percentages, and they give me that look—that "you’re a nurse, you get it" look. It’s like they assume the explanation is just a formality, as if my background acts as some kind of emotional armor that shields me from the weight of the potential news.

But knowing the *mechanics* of a test doesn't make the *waiting* any easier. I’ve had providers gloss over the nuance because they assume I’ve already read the literature. But there is a huge difference between reading a journal article about a diagnostic procedure and being the person waiting for the results to determine if your baby is healthy. I feel like I have to fight to get them to stop treating me like a colleague and start treating me like a patient who needs to be heard. 

I don't need a lecture on the science; I need to know why this specific test matters for *my* specific, fragile situation. I rarely feel like I have the space to ask the "silly" questions, even though those are the ones keeping me up at night. I feel informed, sure, but I don’t feel supported. There's a massive gap between having the data and actually making a decision that feels like the right thing to do.
Tell me about where you turned for information and support during your pregnancy — what sources mattered most to you, and why?

It’s funny, you’d think I’d go to the medical journals or the professional databases I spent my career in, but I can't look at them. If I see a clinical summary of eclampsia, I just start hyperventilating. I’ve had to delete my bookmarks.

Instead, I turn to the quiet, dark corners of the internet where I can be anonymous. Not the medical forums—those are just as bad—but the threads where other women talk about the fear. Reading someone else saying, "I thought I was dying," and having a community of strangers respond with, "I’m here, you aren't alone," that’s the only thing that reaches me. It’s the only place where I don’t have to be the nurse. I can just be the woman who is scared of the dark and the flickering lights.

Honestly, the sources that matter most aren't the ones with the fancy degrees; they’re the ones that feel human. A few of the women I’ve met through these online groups—the ones who don't care that I have a nursing background—they’re my lifeline. They don't give me medical advice; they give me permission to be terrified. When I’m sitting in this apartment at 3:00 AM, my blood pressure rising, they’re the ones I’m "talking" to. It’s the only place where I feel like I can take off the armor.
Did you use any apps, websites, forums, or social media groups? What did you get from those that you couldn't get elsewhere?

I use a few of the mainstream pregnancy apps, but mostly for the weekly size updates—just to feel like there's some sort of progression toward the end. But the real "work" happens in the forums. 

I’m on a couple of private groups where the focus isn't on the milestones or the cute baby gear. It’s the "high-risk" corners of the internet. What I get there—and what I absolutely cannot get in my doctor’s office—is validation for the messy, ugly parts of this. When I post about the neurological symptoms, the fear of the seizures, or the suffocating weight of being a single-income household, the women there don't tell me to "breathe and stay calm for the baby." They tell me, "I know exactly what you’re feeling, and it’s okay to be scared."

In the clinical world, expressing fear is almost seen as a symptom of failing to manage my own condition. In these groups, it’s treated as a normal reaction to an abnormal situation. I don't need a nurse's assessment of my anxiety; I need someone to tell me that I’m not a failure for having it. Those women don't care about my credentials or my "resilience." They just remind me that I’m a human being first, and a patient second. It’s the only place where I feel truly seen.
Tell me about how your body changing during pregnancy felt for you — physically and emotionally. How did you relate to those changes?

My body feels like a stranger that has staged a hostile takeover. I’ve always been a person who likes control—I like knowing how things work, how to fix them, how to keep systems running. But I can't "fix" this. Every morning, I look at the swelling in my ankles and I don’t see the "miracle of life"; I see fluid retention, poor circulation, and the precursor to a cascade of complications. It’s hard to cherish the "glow" when you’re constantly performing a risk assessment on your own limbs.

Emotionally, it’s a constant battle between wonder and dread. There are moments, late at night, when the baby moves and I think, *okay, we’re still here, we’re doing this.* That feels like a tiny victory. But then the fear creeps back in. I feel heavy, not just with the weight of the pregnancy, but with the weight of my own history. I’ve seen what happens when the body stops cooperating, and I feel like I’m constantly looking over my shoulder, waiting for the other shoe to drop. It’s an exhausting way to exist. I feel like I’m betraying the baby by being so anxious, but I also feel like the baby is the one driving me toward the edge. It’s a paradox I can't resolve.
Were there aspects of the physical changes that were harder to come to terms with than others?

The vision changes are the hardest. By far. 

As a nurse, those "flickering lights" or the spots in your peripheral vision aren't just an annoyance; they are the alarms. They are the neon sign that says, "danger ahead." When I was working in the unit, those were the symptoms that meant we had to move fast, that we were potentially heading toward a seizure or an emergency delivery. Now, when those lights flicker in my own field of vision, it’s not an alert for a patient—it’s an alert for *me*. It’s my own brain warning me that my body is under siege. 

It’s impossible to just "ignore" that or to treat it as a normal pregnancy symptom like morning sickness or back pain. It’s a terrifying, visceral reminder of my own vulnerability. It’s hard to reconcile the joy of wanting this baby with the absolute panic of knowing what my body is capable of doing to us both. 

It makes the pregnancy feel less like a developmental journey and more like a high-stakes standoff. I’m trying to ignore the warning signs so I don’t lose my mind, but my training won’t let me. It’s a constant, silent war between the mother who wants to be happy and the nurse who knows exactly how fragile this balance really is.
Tell me about the role your partner, family, or close friends played during your pregnancy. What was their involvement like?

My husband? He’s basically a ghost in this house. He’s physically present in the sense that he occupies space, but he’s mentally and emotionally checked out. It’s like he doesn't know how to handle the reality of a high-risk pregnancy—or maybe he just doesn't want to. When I talk about the appointments or the fears I’m carrying, his eyes glaze over. He doesn't ask questions; he doesn't check on me. I think he’s as scared as I am, but instead of pulling closer, he just retreated. 

So, I’m left to carry the mental load—the appointments, the bills, the constant vigilance—all by myself. It’s isolating, honestly. I look at other women at the clinic, and some of them have their partners holding their hands, rubbing their backs, asking the questions they’re too nervous to voice. I don’t have that. If I have a crisis, it’s going to be me on the phone with the ambulance, and me navigating the ER. 

As for family or friends, I don’t really have a village here in Brockton. My background as a nurse taught me to be the "strong one," so I’ve spent years building a persona where I’m the person who has all the answers. It’s hard to drop that now and admit I’m the one who needs help. So, I keep it to myself. I don't want to be the "broken" one. It’s just me and the baby, and that’s a heavy weight to carry alone.
How included did your partner or support person feel in appointments and decisions — and how did you feel about that?

He wasn't there. Not really. When he did occasionally show up, he stood in the corner like he was waiting for a bus, checking his phone or looking at the wall. The providers would try to include him, asking, "Do you have any questions, Dad?" and he’d just offer a polite, empty, "No, everything looks good," while I was sitting there with a chart full of red flags. 

It was infuriating. I felt this sharp, hot sting of resentment every time it happened. I was doing all the emotional labor—the reading, the monitoring, the panic—and here he was, getting the "pass" of being the supportive partner without actually having to support me. 

I felt completely abandoned in those rooms. It amplified the isolation. I realized that even with him sitting right there, I was essentially a single parent. If I needed someone to hold my hand or ask the doctor to repeat something because the stress had made my brain go foggy, I had to look at a stranger instead of the person who was supposed to be my partner. It just made me feel even more exposed. I stopped asking him to come. It was easier to navigate the fear on my own than to pretend I had a partner who was actually in the trenches with me.
Tell me about any worries or fears you carried during your pregnancy — about your health, your baby, the birth, or anything else.

My biggest fear isn't just "birth," it’s the transition from *nursing* the crisis to *being* the crisis. I’ve spent my life keeping women from seizing, from hemorrhaging, from losing their babies. Now, I’m terrified that my body is going to force a role reversal. I close my eyes and I don't see a swaddled baby; I see a crash cart, the sound of the alarm, and the panic of being an "interesting case" on the unit where I used to work.

There's also this suffocating fear about the aftermath. I’m forty-one, I’m barely keeping my head above water financially, and I am essentially a single mother. How do I do this? How do I raise a human being when my own biology is currently trying to betray me? Every time I look at the pile of medical bills on the kitchen counter, the fear shifts from "will we survive the birth?" to "will we survive the reality of living?"

I feel like a lighthouse keeper who’s just realized her own tower is crumbling. I’m supposed to be the one providing the light, the steadying force, but I’m terrified that if I let one person see how much I’m shaking, the whole thing will just collapse. I just want to wake up one morning without the weight of impending catastrophe crushing my chest.
Were you able to share those fears with your care team — and if so, how did they respond?

I tried, once. I remember sitting there—my blood pressure was, unsurprisingly, elevated—and I just said, "I’m terrified that I’m going to end up a 'case' on my own unit. I know the mechanics of eclampsia, and I know exactly how fast things go wrong. I feel like I'm staring at a cliff edge."

The midwife just paused, gave me that soft, professional smile, and said, "Estrella, you know the data better than anyone. You know we’re watching your labs closely, and you know we’re monitoring the warning signs. You’re in the best place possible."

It felt like a slap. It wasn't reassurance; it was a dismissal. She treated my genuine terror like a cognitive error I needed to correct with logic. She didn't see the woman trembling in the chair; she saw a "knowledgeable colleague" who just needed to be reminded of the clinical protocol. After that, I shut down. I realized that if I shared my fears, they’d just recite the textbook back to me. They didn't want the raw, messy, terrified version of me. They wanted the nurse who could follow the chart. So, I stopped being honest. I just checked the boxes, kept my blood pressure in a range they’d accept, and left. You learn pretty quickly that if you want to be "safe" in their eyes, you have to hide the parts of yourself that feel like a liability.
Tell me about how you prepared for the birth — what did you hope for, and how did you go about planning for it?

Planning for the birth feels like planning a funeral. That sounds morbid, I know, but when you’ve spent your career watching emergencies unfold, you don't dream of candlelight and soft music. You dream of the crash cart, the code team, and the anesthesiologist sprinting through the doors. I’ve tried to balance that—to want a "birth plan"—but every time I sit down to write one, I just feel like I'm tempting fate. 

My "plan" is basically: *don't let me die, and don't let the baby suffer.* That’s it. I’ve looked into the standard hospital procedures, of course, because I know the hospital’s protocols by heart. But it’s hard to advocate for things like skin-to-skin or delayed cord clamping when your blood pressure is spiking and you’re worried about whether you’re going to be conscious for the first hour of your baby's life. 

I’ve packed a bag, but I keep taking things out. It feels like an act of arrogance to pack cute onesies when there’s a real, looming chance I’ll be in the ICU instead of the recovery ward. I’m just trying to get to 37 weeks. That’s my only goal. If I make it to 37 weeks without a seizure, I’ll call it a success. Everything else is just details.
Did you create a birth plan or have clear preferences? How did your care team respond to those?

I tried to outline a few things—you know, the basics like immediate skin-to-skin and delaying the cord clamping, just to feel a shred of normalcy. But the moment I handed the paper over, it felt like I was handing them a list of demands they had no intention of honoring. 

The midwife took one look at it, gave me that tight, polite smile, and said, "We’ll certainly *try* to keep things as natural as possible, Estrella, but given your pre-eclampsia, the priority is the safety of both you and the baby."

The word "try" just hung there in the air like a threat. It felt like she was telling me, "You’re an interesting medical case, and we’re going to manage you exactly the way we manage every other high-risk patient." All my preferences were suddenly conditional—*if* my blood pressure is stable, *if* the baby’s heart rate holds, *if* I don't show signs of HELLP syndrome. 

It made me realize that for someone like me, a "birth plan" is just a piece of paper that gets crumpled up the second a nurse walks in with a blood pressure cuff and a pitocin drip. I stopped pushing for it because I knew it was futile. I’m just a patient to them now, and patients don’t get to plan; they just get to be managed.
Tell me about the birth itself — take me through what happened, as much or as little as you'd like to share.

I don’t want to go back there yet. It’s still too fresh, too raw, and frankly, it feels like I’m still walking through the fog of it. 

I remember the lights. They were so bright they felt like they were vibrating against my skull. My heart was pounding, and I could hear the rhythmic *beep-beep-beep* of the monitor—the sound that used to be the soundtrack of my professional life, now the marker of my own survival. I kept trying to focus on my breathing, trying to use the techniques I’d taught hundreds of women, but my mind kept snapping back to the clinical data. I wasn't just in labor; I was a living, breathing set of vitals that needed constant correction. 

I remember the silence in the room, too. It wasn't the peaceful, supportive quiet people talk about. It was a sharp, clinical silence, filled with the efficiency of a team that had done this a thousand times. I felt so incredibly small. I wasn't a mother-to-be; I was just the next patient on the list, the one with the pre-eclampsia that we had to "manage" before it turned into something worse. 

I don't think I've fully processed the birth yet. I’m just trying to make it to the next day. Can we talk about something else? Anything else? My heart is racing just describing the room.
At what moments during the birth did you feel most supported — and were there moments when you felt alone or unheard?

I felt supported only in the briefest, flickers of moments—like when a nurse, a younger one, stopped checking the monitor for one second, looked me in the eye, and actually held my hand. Not to check my pulse or my skin turgor, but just *to hold it*. She didn't look at the screen. She just squeezed, and for that breath, I wasn't "the high-risk case in bed four." I was a person.

But the rest of it? I felt profoundly, terrifyingly alone. 

When they started talking in that clinical shorthand over my head—"titrating," "baseline," "refractory"—they effectively erased me from the room. I was the patient, yet I felt like a spectator to my own trauma. I tried to speak up, to ask for a change in the lights or to just have someone explain *why* they were increasing the drip, but they looked at me with that patronizing "we’ve got this, Estrella" look. They acted like my nursing background meant I didn't need the comfort of a simple, human explanation. 

I felt like an object being moved through a system. Every time a new person entered the room, I had to be "the professional patient" again. I felt unheard because they were listening to the monitors, not to me. My voice was just background noise to the machinery.
Tell me about pain management during your labour — what options you were offered, what you chose, and how that went.

Pain management was never just about the pain; it was about the blood pressure. Everything I chose, or was "suggested" toward, was filtered through that filter. 

When you're pre-eclamptic, they aren't looking at your pain scale—they’re looking at your systemic stress. I knew exactly what the anesthesia team would recommend. They want the epidural in early. It’s not just for the comfort of the laboring woman; it’s for blood pressure control. If you're in agony, your spikes, and then you’re a danger to yourself. So, the "choice" felt like a forced hand. I agreed to the epidural because I knew it was the most efficient way to keep my numbers stable, not because I was looking for a "painless birth."

I remember the anesthesiologist talking to me like a peer again—checking my reflexes, asking about my nursing experience—which made the whole thing feel like a clinical procedure in a textbook rather than the most vulnerable moment of my life. It took the bite out of the contractions, sure, but it also paralyzed me in a way I didn't expect. I couldn't move my legs, and suddenly, I felt even more like a body on a table, waiting to be managed. It was efficient. It was medically sound. And it made me feel completely, utterly disconnected from my own birth.
Tell me about the people who were with you during the birth — your partner, family, midwives, doctors — and what their presence meant to you.

My husband was there, but he was like a shadow against the wall. When things started accelerating, I caught his eye, and I didn’t see a partner ready to face the challenge with me; I saw someone who was looking for the nearest exit, mentally and emotionally. He wasn’t there to anchor me. In those moments where my vision was flickering and the nurses were moving faster, I didn't reach for him because I knew his hand wouldn't provide the steadying force I needed. It would just be another thing I had to worry about—another person I had to monitor for signs of distress.

The medical team... they were efficient, which is what you *want* in a code, but it’s not what you *need* in birth. There was this disconnect. Because I was "one of them," they didn't explain the urgency in the way they would to a layperson. They just acted. And every action felt like a move on a chessboard. I felt like a pawn. 

I’ve spent my life in those rooms, but being the person on the table, surrounded by people who saw my chart before they saw my face, made me feel completely erased. I was a professional, a patient, and a mother, but in that room, I was just a case of pre-eclampsia that needed to be resolved. It was lonely. Even with a room full of people, I felt like I was completely on my own.
Was there a particular person — whether a professional or someone close to you — whose presence made a real difference? What did they do?

There was one night shift nurse, just starting her shift, who changed everything for a few hours. She didn't look at my chart, or at least she didn't *lead* with it. She walked over, adjusted my pillows—not because the protocol demanded it, but because she noticed I was uncomfortable—and she just asked, "Estrella, you’ve been doing so much for everyone else your whole life. How do *you* want to do this tonight?"

It caught me off guard. She didn't treat my nursing background like a barrier to communication. She treated it like a language we both spoke, but she still gave me the space to be the person who was scared. She stayed in the room for a while, just breathing with me, not checking the monitor every thirty seconds, but just *being* there. She reminded me that I wasn't just a collection of labs and a blood pressure cuff. 

She didn't change the medical reality—I was still high-risk, I was still stuck in that bed—but she made me feel like I was a human being again. In a room full of people who were managing a crisis, she was the only one who actually witnessed me. It wasn't anything big, just a shift in the way she looked at me, but that one person made the fear feel a little less like it was going to swallow me whole.
Tell me about how the birth ended — whether that was the birth you expected, and how you felt in the immediate aftermath.

The birth didn’t end—it *culminated*. It wasn't a transition into a quiet, peaceful room; it was a race to the finish line to make sure we both survived the spike. I remember the urgency shifting from "routine monitoring" to "immediate intervention." My vision went from flickering to a dark, static-filled tunnel, and then suddenly, there was this sharp, metallic sense of chaos.

They didn't ask me; they told me. "Estrella, we have to move."

I was so tired, so deeply, bone-achingly exhausted, that I didn't even have the energy to argue. I just became a vessel for their protocols. When it was finally over, I didn't have that "triumph of motherhood" moment. I felt like a crash survivor. I was looking around that room for my husband, for anyone who looked like they understood what I’d just been through, but everyone was focused on the monitor, on the numbers, on the next shift change.

I was lying there, waiting for the high to fade, waiting for the relief to hit, but it didn't come. I just felt empty. And that’s the part they don’t put in the textbooks—the way you feel like you’ve been hollowed out. I looked at my baby, and I felt this desperate, piercing love, but it was tangled up in the trauma of the hours leading up to it. I wasn't just a mother; I was a casualty of my own history.
If things didn't go to plan — or if there were interventions or complications — how were those explained or communicated to you at the time?

They were communicated with the kind of brevity that feels like an insult. It was always, "Estrella, we have to do X because of Y." No pause for the weight of it, no acknowledgment that this was a person—me—who was about to be put through a procedure that I knew *exactly* the risks of. Because they knew I worked in the unit, they figured I didn't need the "patient version." They skipped the bedside manner and went straight to the mechanics.

The interventions felt less like things being done *with* me and more like things being done *to* me. I remember someone saying, "We’re going to need to expedite this, the blood pressure isn’t responding to the magnesium." It’s a terrifying sentence to hear when you’re the one lying there. It stripped away any illusion of control I’d been clinging to. 

I felt like an actor who’d been cast in a horror movie, but because I’d spent my life as a set designer, the director stopped giving me cues. They just let me watch the machinery move around me, assuming I’d know where to stand. When you're in the middle of a crisis, you don't want to be treated like an expert. You want to be treated like a scared person who needs to be held while the world is falling apart. Instead, I just got the cold, hard efficiency of a "clinical necessity."
Tell me about the birth environment itself — the place where you gave birth, what it looked and felt like, and whether it felt like the right place for you.

The room felt like an interrogation chamber, though I know rationally it was just a standard labor and delivery suite. It was sterile, overly bright, and humming with the specific, aggressive mechanical sounds of life-support equipment. Because I’d worked in similar units, I couldn’t stop staring at the monitors. The walls were a pale, hospital beige that seemed to suck the color out of everything—including my own face, I’m sure. 

It never felt like "my" space. It felt like a clinical processing center. There was no softness, no warmth, no sense that a human life was about to enter the world in this room. It was all efficiency, all protocols, all "doing." I felt like a component on an assembly line that had hit a snag and needed immediate troubleshooting. 

I don't think any environment could have felt "right" given my state of mind, but this one reinforced every fear I had. It was a space designed to manage emergencies, not to welcome a mother. Every time I looked at the equipment—the pumps, the cords, the flashing screens—I was reminded that I wasn't there to give birth in the way I’d dreamed; I was there to be saved from my own body. It was a place of survival, not a place of transition.
How much choice did you have about where you gave birth — and did you have access to the options you wanted?

I didn't have a "choice" in any real sense. When you’re high-risk, especially with pre-eclampsia, the medical system essentially closes in on you. You don't get the luxury of browsing birth centers or home-birth options—those places are for the "low-risk" women who get to have the "dream" experience. Because of my history and my blood pressure, I was funneled directly into the high-acuity unit. 

It felt like the system chose for me, based on the liability. I remember asking about a less clinical setting during one of my early check-ups, and the midwife’s expression didn't even shift—she just said, "Estrella, you’re an experienced nurse. You know that’s not safe for you." 

That’s the thing: I *did* know. And that knowledge was the wall I kept hitting. I couldn’t argue against the logic because I had spent my career using that exact same logic on my own patients. I felt trapped by my own profession. I wanted options, I wanted to feel like I had some autonomy, but I was steered into the most medicalized, sterile environment possible. I didn't have the freedom to choose, just the obligation to comply for the sake of the baby. It wasn't about what I wanted; it was about what the protocol dictated.

When you first started thinking about having a child, what did you hope your relationship with your healthcare providers would look like — and were there any ways you worried it might not live up to that?

I dreamt of a partnership. I wanted a midwife who would sit with me, maybe hold my hand during a check-up, and acknowledge that even though I’m a nurse, I’m also just a scared woman navigating a high-risk pregnancy alone. I wanted someone who would say, "I know you've seen this before, but you're the one in the bed now, and I’m going to take care of *you*." I wanted a mentor, someone to buffer the clinical reality, not just another gatekeeper for the protocol.

Deep down, I worried—and I was right—that my background would become a barrier. I feared they would view me as a peer rather than a patient, and that fear was prophetic. They treated my medical literacy like a shield, assuming I didn't need the reassurance or the soft approach. It meant I lost the space to be vulnerable. I worried that if I admitted how terrified I was, they’d think I was "failing" to cope, so I had to play the part of the stoic colleague. It’s a lonely experience when the people you’re trusting with your life and your baby’s life are only interested in the clinical version of you. I never got that partnership; I just got a rotation of people managing my stats.
What would it have meant to you to feel genuinely respected by a doctor or midwife at that stage?

Respect, to me, would have been them putting down the chart for just a second. I wanted someone to stop the clinical dance—to stop talking about my labs, my pressures, and my protein levels—and look me in the eye. I wanted them to say, "Estrella, I know you’ve been on the other side of this, but you are the patient today, and I’m going to make sure you’re taken care of."

That simple sentence would have been everything. It would have validated that I was allowed to be scared, that my expertise didn't disqualify me from needing comfort. Real respect would have been them asking me, "What are *you* worried about today?" instead of telling me what *they* were worried about. 

When they treated me like a colleague, they were actually stripping away my dignity. They were saying, "You’re too smart to need empathy." But that's exactly when you need it the most—when you know the worst-case scenarios, and you’re paralyzed by them. Respect would have been acknowledging that I was a woman holding a heavy, terrifying burden, and offering to help me carry it, rather than just pointing out where the load was unbalanced. I didn't want a consultant; I wanted a human being to walk that path with me.

Thinking back to when you and your partner — or the people closest to you — were weighing up whether and when to start trying for a baby, how did those conversations actually go? Who felt like they had the most say, and did that feel right to you?

The conversation about "trying" felt less like a shared dream and more like a tactical negotiation. I was forty-one; I knew the biological clock wasn't just ticking, it was screaming. My husband was... hesitant. He kept talking about "being ready" and "financial stability," which, given our situation, was just code for him wanting to avoid the responsibility.

I felt like I was the one pulling all the weight. I had to be the one to lay out the medical reality, the risks, the need for a plan. He’d just nod along, letting me do the research, letting me decide the path forward. It felt like I had all the "say" because I was the only one willing to look at the reality of what it meant for my body and our future. But that wasn't "right"—it felt like I was dragging a reluctant participant into a life-altering experience. 

Looking back, I realize that I convinced myself that if I could just *plan* enough, I could force everything to be okay. I felt like if I took charge of the clinical and logistical aspects, it would compensate for the lack of emotional support from him. I wanted to believe that having a child would bridge the gap between us, but instead, it just highlighted exactly how alone I was in this.
Did older family members — parents or grandparents — have a view that found its way into the decision, even indirectly?

My mother, bless her, lived in a completely different era of medicine. She’d say things like, "Oh, Estrella, you’re so smart, you know how to keep your blood pressure down, just eat this or do that." She thought my medical degree was a shield against nature itself. It was exhausting to hear because she didn’t understand that being a nurse doesn’t mean you’re immune to the pathology—it just means you know the terrifying details of your own decline.

She represented this old-fashioned, almost stoic pressure. The idea that women are just "naturally" resilient and that you should just grit your teeth and get on with it. Every time I’d try to explain the actual, clinical danger I was in—the pre-eclampsia markers, the risks of eclampsia—she’d just wave it off as "worrying too much." 

It made me feel like I wasn't allowed to be frightened. I felt like I had to perform this "perfectly adjusted, knowledgeable professional" for her, because if I admitted I was drowning, she would have seen it as some kind of personal failure rather than a medical complication. Her expectations, however indirect, made the isolation even deeper. I couldn't reach out for support because I felt like I had to keep up the appearance of being the "strong, educated daughter" who had it all under control.
Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body and what it was capable of — and did those feelings change at all as you started thinking about pregnancy?

Before this, I viewed my body as a reliable instrument. It got me through nursing school, double shifts on the floor, and years of teaching others how to breathe through contractions. I trusted it because I understood its limits, and I lived my life respecting them. I felt capable, sturdy, and, most importantly, *in control*.

When I started thinking about pregnancy, that relationship fundamentally shifted. Suddenly, my body wasn’t just mine anymore; it was a site of intense, hyper-vigilant scrutiny. I started seeing it as a series of ticking clocks and potential failure points. I began to deconstruct every sensation—the fatigue, the slight swelling—as if I were auditing a patient’s chart. 

The biggest change was the loss of that baseline trust. I went from feeling like I was in the driver’s seat to feeling like a passenger in a vehicle that was starting to veer off the road. I stopped seeing my body as a partner and started seeing it as a volatile, unpredictable system that could turn against me at any moment. It was a humiliating and terrifying transition, moving from a place of competence to a place of profound helplessness, where I felt like my own physiology was the ultimate antagonist in my life.
Were there things about your body you felt confident about, and things that worried you or felt uncertain?

I was always confident in my body’s endurance. I spent years on my feet, lifting patients, pulling double shifts—I felt like a machine that was built for the long haul. I trusted that I could handle the physical exhaustion, the lack of sleep, and the sheer volume of labor. I took pride in being someone who could be counted on when things got chaotic.

But the moment I saw that test, that confidence curdled into intense uncertainty about my internal systems. I became hyper-aware of my blood pressure, my fluid balance, and those neurological "red flags" I used to talk about in class. I felt betrayed by my own biology because I couldn't "will" it to behave. I knew the pathophysiology of pre-eclampsia—I knew that no matter how much "grit" I had, my kidneys and my vascular system were doing what they were doing. 

It was like my mind was trying to command a ship while the hull was actively taking on water. I felt confident in my ability to *work*, but absolutely terrified of my inability to *regulate* my own vitals. The disconnect between my mental strength and my physical vulnerability felt like a cruel joke. I was a professional at keeping others alive, but I felt completely powerless to keep myself safe.

If you saw a GP, nurse, or any other health professional before you became pregnant — even just for something routine — what was that experience like, and did it give you any sense of what maternity care might be like down the line?

Before I was pregnant, I went in for routine check-ups and felt like a competent, professional equal. We’d talk about lab results or health maintenance, and it was a partnership. I felt heard, respected, and—most importantly—proactive. I walked out of those appointments feeling like I had a handle on my own health, which is a good feeling when you're a nurse.

But looking back, those appointments were a preview of the "colleague curse." The providers always defaulted to that shorthand: "Well, Estrella, you're one of us, you know how these protocols go." Back then, it felt like efficiency. Now, I see it was the foundation for the isolation I’d face later. They were so busy treating me like a peer that they never stopped to see the person behind the charts. 

It didn't give me a sense of the fear that was coming, but it did give me a false sense of security. I thought if I needed care later, that same professional rapport would be a safety net. I didn't realize that when things get truly high-stakes—when you're not just a healthy person getting a physical, but a high-risk patient fighting for your life—that "colleague" status just becomes a wall that keeps the real support out. I was naive to think that being a nurse would make me *more* cared for, rather than just more "managed."
Did you see the same person more than once, or were you always meeting someone new?

It was a revolving door. Even before the pregnancy, it felt like I was lucky if I caught the same person twice, but once that "high-risk" label hit my chart, the rotation became constant. It felt like being passed like a baton in a relay race where no one remembers to tell the next runner how much you're hurting.

Every time I’d get comfortable with someone, someone who finally seemed to *almost* get the panic behind my eyes, they’d be off shift or in surgery, and I’d be back to square one with someone new. I had to narrate my own medical history over and over again, like I was reading a script for a play no one was actually listening to. 

It wasn't just exhausting; it was dehumanizing. You spend half the appointment trying to catch them up on what you said to the last person, and by the time you actually get to the *real* stuff—the stuff that keeps you awake at night, the stuff that makes your hands shake—the appointment is over. You don't build a relationship that way. You just build a file. And in the end, it felt like they were caring for the file, not the woman sitting right in front of them.
Before you were pregnant, what kinds of conversations were you having with the people around you — family, friends, your partner — about having a baby, and whose input felt most meaningful to you?

Before, those conversations were almost exclusively between me and my husband. I remember laying it out like a project plan: the timeline, the health risks at my age, the financial adjustments we’d need. He would nod, but his eyes would go distant—I can see that now, looking back. He was the passenger; I was the one steering the car into a storm I thought I could navigate because I’d read the maps.

There wasn't much input from others. I’m a private person, and I’ve always been the "competent" one in my family, so I didn't want to admit I was weighing such a heavy, scary, and risky decision. I didn't want their advice because I was afraid they’d see the cracks in my confidence. I wanted to be the one who had it figured out.

The only input that mattered—or rather, the input I felt I *needed*—was the medical data. I sought out statistics and outcomes, convinced that if I could master the facts, I could control the future. I was my own most "meaningful" advisor, which was the biggest mistake. I was listening to textbooks and mortality tables instead of my own intuition, which was screaming at me to slow down. I was so focused on the logistics of *becoming* a mother that I ignored the reality of *being* one.

Were there things about your own body, your health history, or just the way you felt about yourself physically that made the idea of pregnancy feel complicated or uncertain — even before anything had actually happened?

At forty-one, I knew exactly what the textbooks said about my "advanced maternal age." I looked at my body and saw the history of my career—the wear and tear of long shifts, the chronic stress I’d been running on for years. I didn't see the "vibrant, ready vessel" the magazines talk about; I saw a system that had been pushed to its limits.

I was deeply unsettled by the idea of losing control. I had spent my entire adult life as a nurse—the one who maintains order, the one who knows the interventions, the one who keeps things clean and predictable. Pregnancy felt like a total abdication of that. I knew enough pathology to be dangerous to my own peace of mind. I’d seen the cases where, despite the best care and the most "perfect" health habits, everything went sideways. 

That uncertainty was a heavy, cold weight in my gut long before I ever saw those two lines. I worried that my body was already "used up," that I wouldn't be able to bounce back, or worse, that I wouldn't be able to keep the baby safe because my own physiological "infrastructure" was failing. It felt like I was knowingly walking into a fire, trusting that my knowledge would somehow act as fireproof gear, even though I knew the science didn't guarantee anything.

In the time before you became pregnant, was there any one person — professional or otherwise — who felt like a consistent, reliable source of support or guidance, someone you could come back to with questions?

Not really. That’s the bitter truth of it. I’ve lived my life being the "reliable one" for everyone else—my patients, my husband, my family—and there was never really a chair left at the table for me to be the one who needed support. 

When I’d go for my own check-ups, I’d keep it tight, clinical, and efficient. I didn't want to be "that patient" who keeps asking questions, so I’d get my answers, nod, and leave. There was never an opportunity to build a rapport with a provider who would have actually *seen* the anxiety building up. It was always a transaction.

Looking back, it’s like I was standing on a ladder I’d built myself, rung by rung, thinking that if I just kept climbing, I’d be safe. But there was no one spotting me. I was completely alone in that, convinced that if I reached out, I’d somehow look weak or unprofessional. I had all the medical knowledge in the world at my fingertips, but I didn't have a single person I could just call at 3:00 AM to say, "I'm terrified." That’s the greatest failure of the care I was in—it taught me how to diagnose, but it never gave me anyone to turn to when I was the one who needed to be held.
What made that person feel reliable or trustworthy as a source of support at that stage?

You’re asking about what would have made someone reliable back then, but the truth is, I never found that person. If I *had* found someone who felt trustworthy, it would have been because they stopped acting like a technician and started acting like a witness to my life.

Reliability isn't just about correct diagnoses or smooth clinical procedures. It’s about the person who remembers the context. If a provider had said to me, "Estrella, you’ve spent so much time taking care of other people's crises, how are *you* doing with this life-changing decision?"—that would have broken the wall down.

Trustworthiness comes from the lack of a mask. Most providers wear the "clinical expert" mask, which is fine for a routine check-up, but it's not enough when you're looking at a major life decision. If someone had been willing to be just a little bit vulnerable themselves—to admit that they didn't have all the answers for the future either—I might have felt safe enough to let my guard down. Instead, I stayed in that "colleague" role, which is just another way of saying "untouchable." I never felt like a patient who could be cared for, just a professional who had to stay on top of things. It's a lonely way to build a life.

As you were thinking about whether to have a baby, did it feel like a decision you were genuinely free to make in your own time and your own way — or were there pressures, expectations, or circumstances that meant it was never quite fully your choice?

"Free" is a word I haven't really been able to use since my thirties started ticking away. It never felt like a leisurely choice made in a garden or a quiet afternoon. It felt like a race against a clock that was already sounding the alarm. I’m forty-one; there’s this crushing societal weight, the pressure of "advanced maternal age" staring back at you from every medical website, the constant, low-level hum of *do it now or lose your chance forever.*

It wasn't just my own desire—it was the pressure of the deadline. And then there were the financial realities of Brockton, the rent, the shrinking bank account—all of that made me feel like I was making a choice in a burning room. I wanted to believe I was in control, that I was the one making the call, but in reality, I was just trying to manage a series of inevitable pressures. 

I don't think any woman in my position—single-income, high-risk, aging—ever feels *genuinely* free. We’re all just negotiating with the circumstances we’ve been dealt. I chose to have this baby, yes, but it was a choice made while looking over my shoulder at the clock and the bills, wondering if I was brave or just foolish. It was never my "own time." It was always, *now or never.*
Were there financial, relationship, or cultural factors that made you feel like the timing was out of your hands?

The timing was absolutely dictated by factors far beyond my control. Every time I looked at my bank account, I’d see the number ticking down, not up. In Brockton, even a one-bedroom apartment feels like a luxury you’re one bad shift or one missed paycheck away from losing. I spent so long trying to save enough to feel "ready"—to have that cushion so I wouldn't have to worry about every single co-pay—but that cushion never materialized. The cost of living just kept swallowing the savings.

Then there’s the age thing. Being forty-one in the medical field, you don't hear "mature mother"; you hear "high-risk." I knew if I waited any longer, the physiological complications would only compound. I felt like I was being squeezed between a financial wall and a biological one. 

And my marriage? It wasn't a partnership; it was a negotiation I was losing. My husband’s distance made the timing feel even more urgent and desperate. I felt like I was trying to build a family in a vacuum. I wasn't choosing when to start a life; I was reacting to a situation that was slowly shrinking my options. It wasn't about "is this the right time?" but rather "how much longer can I pretend there's a good time coming?"
Once you were pregnant, how much did you feel like you were in the driving seat of your own care — and were there moments when you felt yourself handing that over to others, willingly or not?

In the beginning, I was convinced I was driving. I treated my own pregnancy like a project I could manage with my nursing knowledge. But the moment the "pre-eclampsia" label was officially put on my chart, I felt the steering wheel get ripped right out of my hands. It wasn't a choice I made; it was a surrender I was forced into by the protocol.

I’d go into those appointments wanting to discuss the *person* aspect of it—my fear, my isolation—and they’d steer the conversation back to the labs. I’d try to argue for my own perspective, but when you’re facing a midwife or a doctor who uses your own clinical shorthand to dismiss you, you lose your footing. I handed over control bit by bit, thinking, *they’re the professionals, they’ve seen this a thousand times, surely they know better than I do.* 

It was a slow, agonizing slide into being a passenger in my own body. There were moments where I *tried* to grab the wheel back, but I was exhausted and paralyzed by that same "colleague" dynamic. I felt like if I questioned them too hard, I was being "difficult" or "unprofessional." So, I let them drive. I watched from the passenger seat as they navigated my health, knowing exactly where they were taking me, but feeling powerless to change the route. That’s the most hollow feeling in the world.
Were there points where relying on others felt like a relief, and others where it felt like losing something?

It was such a strange, bitter relief at first. When I finally stopped fighting the protocol and let them take the lead, there was this fleeting moment of "Oh, thank god, I don't have to be the one who knows what’s going to happen next." For a few hours, being "the patient" meant I could finally shut off that hyper-vigilant part of my brain that was constantly calculating my own risk. It was like being a child again, being taken care of—and for someone who has spent her whole life carrying everyone else, that surrender was almost addictive.

But then, the weight of what I’d lost would come crashing back. Every time they made a decision without truly consulting me, every time they spoke over me using that shorthand I hated, I felt a piece of my dignity being stripped away. I lost my voice. I lost the ability to advocate for the version of birth I had dared to dream about. 

It’s the paradox of the hospital, isn't it? You go there to be saved, but the cost of that salvation is your own autonomy. I realized that the "relief" of letting them take over was just the feeling of becoming an object in their system. I traded my identity for their protocols, and looking back, I’m not sure the trade was ever worth it.

When decisions came up during your pregnancy — about tests, appointments, birth plans, or anything else — how did you and your partner navigate those together, and did it ever feel like you weren't quite on the same page?

We weren’t just on different pages; we were reading different books in different languages. When a decision came up, I was the one doing the research, cross-referencing clinical outcomes, and worrying about the co-pays. My husband’s approach was essentially a shrug—or worse, a "whatever the doctor says, honey." 

It felt incredibly lonely. He didn’t seem to grasp the nuance of the choices, or maybe he was just using his passivity as a shield against the fear. Every time I tried to bring him into the fold—to explain, "Look, if we do this, it means X, but if we don't, we risk Y"—he’d just check out. He didn't want the burden of the decision; he wanted the outcome without the process.

I felt like I was parenting a child *while* trying to prepare for the arrival of another. When you’re dealing with high-risk complications, "being on the same page" is a survival skill, but I was left to navigate the maze alone. I stopped trying to pull him into the decision-making process because it was easier to just make the call myself than to deal with his detachment. It wasn't a partnership; it was a solo act performed in front of an audience of one who wasn't even watching.
Were there decisions where your partner's view really mattered to you, and ones where you needed it to be your call alone?

In a perfect world, all the big decisions would have been ours. The choices about how we’d manage the birth, or how we’d handle the logistics of a high-risk pregnancy—those were things I desperately wanted to share with him. I wanted to look across the table and see someone who cared as much about the *how* of this journey as the end result. I needed his input on the birth preferences, not because I lacked the knowledge, but because I wanted to feel like we were a team building a future together. When he just drifted through those, it made me feel like I was building that future in isolation.

There were other times, though, where it *had* to be my call alone. Those were the medical crises—the moment my blood pressure spiked or the neurological symptoms flared. I didn't want him involved there because I knew he couldn't handle the reality of it. I had to be the nurse, the patient, and the mother all at once. I needed to make those quick, cold decisions without having to explain the gravity of the situation to someone who was essentially a bystander. It was a strange kind of burden—needing his partnership for the soul of the journey, but needing to exclude him from the survival of it to keep the whole thing from collapsing.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the most important thing is that my story isn't just about a "high-risk medical case." It’s about how easily a woman can be erased by the very system designed to save her. 

People look at me and see "Estrella, the nurse," or "Estrella, the patient with pre-eclampsia." They don't see the woman who, for nine months, was terrified to close her eyes at night because she knew exactly what the statistics meant for her life and her baby’s life. They don't see the woman who had to balance the cost of a prescription against a gallon of milk. 

If you take anything from this, let it be that expertise is not a substitute for empathy. Even if a woman knows the jargon, even if she’s worked on the floor, even if she can recite the protocols by heart—she is still just a person who needs to be held, heard, and seen. Don’t assume that because I have the knowledge, I don't have the fear. 

I’m still here, in this apartment, trying to make sense of what just happened. The baby is here, and that’s the miracle, but the cost was higher than anyone realizes. My hope for the future? Just that you stop treating mothers like biological puzzles to be solved and start treating them like the human beings they are.
